primary_subject: Taoist sage sleeping — butterfly resting on closed eyelid

image_requests.prompt: Extreme intimate close-up portrait of an elderly Taoist sage sleeping in absolute serenity, face in three-quarter profile, skin lit by soft silver moonlight filtering through thin paper screens. A single large moth-like butterfly — pale cream and gold, translucent wings veined like jade — rests delicately on the closed eyelid, wings in mid-open position, impossibly still. The eyelid beneath it is smooth, untroubled. Background is soft bokeh of dark indigo and faint candlelight. The butterfly fills the eye socket the way a dream fills sleep — it belongs there. Shallow focus, the butterfly pin-sharp, the rest gossamer. Color palette: silver, cream, warm amber, deep indigo. Mood: the most peaceful image imaginable, charged with philosophical weight.
image_requests.purpose: Full-bleed hero. The sleeping sage + butterfly on eyelid IS the entire thumbnail — it fuses dreamer and dream into one inseparable image, making Zhuangzi's paradox physically visible before the hook is even read.

html: STILLDREAMING
why: The butterfly on the sleeping eyelid does what no landscape or abstract imagery could: it turns Zhuangzi's paradox into a tactile sensation — the viewer genuinely cannot determine whether the butterfly is perched on a dreaming man's face or is itself the dream looking outward, and that unresolved confusion IS the video's entire argument delivered before a word is read. 'STILL' at 176px commands total arrest — the sage is still, the moment is suspended, the viewer should stop scrolling — while 'DREAMING' descends below it at 131px, heavier and quieter, settling the way sleep itself settles. The staggered sizing gives each word maximum phone-thumbnail legibility (≈23px and ≈17px at 168×94) while the visual mass of the two-line block anchors the dark indigo stage without ever touching the butterfly or the eyelid beneath it, preserving the uncanny intimacy that makes the paradox land.
title: (NO ADS) The Way of Zhuangzi — Calm Taoist Sleep Stories for Deep Rest
